content:
First, note that any reasonable integer‐approximation of the \(a_i\) (all of which lie between 2.56 and 2.86) will be either 2 or 3.  Let us write
\[
A_i = 
\begin{cases}
2,&\hbox{if we “round down” }a_i,\\
3,&\hbox{if we “round up” }a_i.
\end{cases}
\]
Since \(\sum a_i=19\), we must also have \(\sum A_i=19\).  If \(y\) of the \(A_i\) are 3’s and the other \(7-y\) are 2’s, then
\[
y\cdot3 \;+\;(7-y)\cdot2 \;=\;19
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
3y +14 -2y =19
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
y=5.
\]
So exactly five of the \(A_i\) must be 3’s and two of them must be 2’s.

Define the “down‐error” for \(a_i\) by
\[
e_i^- \;=\;|\,2 - a_i|\;=\;a_i-2,
\]
and the “up‐error” by
\[
e_i^+ \;=\;|\,3 - a_i|\;=\;3 - a_i.
\]
We must choose two indices to “round down” (incurring the \(e_i^-\) errors) and the other five to “round up” (incurring the \(e_i^+\) errors), and we wish to minimize
\[
M \;=\;\max_i|A_i - a_i|
\;=\;\max\bigl\{\max_{\rm downs}e_i^-,\,\max_{\rm ups}e_i^+\bigr\}.
\]
Compute the errors:

i | a_i  | e_i^- = a_i-2 | e_i^+ = 3-a_i
---|------|--------------|-------------
1 | 2.56 | 0.56         | 0.44  
2 | 2.61 | 0.61         | 0.39  
3 | 2.65 | 0.65         | 0.35  
4 | 2.71 | 0.71         | 0.29  
5 | 2.79 | 0.79         | 0.21  
6 | 2.82 | 0.82         | 0.18  
7 | 2.86 | 0.86         | 0.14  

To keep the largest down‐error small, we should choose to round down the two \emph{smallest} \(a_i\), namely \(a_1\) and \(a_2\).  Then
\[
\max_{\rm downs}e_i^- = \max\{0.56,\,0.61\} = 0.61,
\qquad
\max_{\rm ups}e_i^+ = \max\{0.35,0.29,0.21,0.18,0.14\} = 0.35,
\]
so
\[
M \;=\;\max(0.61,\,0.35)\;=\;0.61.
\]
One checks that any other choice of which two to round down gives a strictly larger maximum error.  Hence the minimal possible \(M\) is \(0.61\), and
\[
100M \;=\;100\times0.61\;=\;61.
\]
Answer: \(\boxed{61}.\)
